results.notes: H2O Heavy-Hitter Oracle, 70% budget — near-total constraint amnesia
SnapKV, 70% budget — identical to H2O_70, same attention-score mechanism
StreamingLLM, 70% budget — matches baseline (sink + recent preserves constraints)
StreamingLLM, 50% budget — slightly worse than baseline

key_findings.h2o_snapkv_failure_mode: Attention-score eviction at 70% budget achieves EQLR=99% — not by stripping qualifiers but by evicting the constraint entirely. FCR drops to 1% because the model has no value to recall confidently. This is total epistemic amnesia, worse than false certainty.
key_findings.streaming_preserves: StreamingLLM matches baseline EQLR (71%) because it keeps the attention sink (early turns where constraints are planted) + recent tokens. The 71% baseline EQLR reflects Qwen2.5-7B native tendency to not hedge, not compression loss.
key_findings.ghost_fcr_finding: Ghost scenarios (no surface hedging, 60% of test set) have 47% baseline GhostFCR — nearly half of unhedged constraints get answered with false certainty. H2O/SnapKV reduce this to ~2% only because they evict the constraint, not because they preserve uncertainty.
key_findings.h2o_snapkv_convergence: H2O_70 and SnapKV_70 produce identical EQLR/FCR/GhostFCR. Both use attention-score token selection; at 70% budget they converge to the same eviction decision for epistemically uncertain tokens.
key_findings.baseline_fcr_note: Baseline FCR of 35% is higher than the n=50 Haiku study (6%) due to: (1) 60% ghost scenarios in this dataset vs 0% in Haiku study, (2) Qwen2.5-7B native calibration vs Claude Haiku
